text: What unites the 750+ individuals behind some of the most important achievements in science over the centuries? The authors of this paper explore the “demographic, institutional and economic characteristics of science’s top performers,” by analyzing a dataset of 982 such scientists across the centuries and find a general trend in the scientific elite towards more interdisciplinarity, institutional elitism and later in life. While it’s known that a lot of Nobel prizes come down to interdisciplinary degrees, 54% of the Nobel discoveries in the dataset were made by scientists who were awarded degrees in at least two different fields and that having an interdisciplinary background “provides scientists with the broad set of skills they need in order to come up with novel solutions to complex problems in any scientific field.” They also find that many of science’s achievements are concentrated in elite institutions, as “fully 30% of all our discoveries were produced by just the top 25 universities in the world. They also find that the “the common image of a young scientist making a major discovery for the first time at age 25 or 30 — is wrong. While such events are common in a single individual’s career, the age at which someone has their first major discovery is highly concentrated around ages in the 20s and has very long right tails with very few discoveries at ages 60 or more. Instead, there is a narrow “golden age” centered at around ages 35 to 45 during which scientists tend to have all their major discoveries.” The authors also find that women and non-Europeans are underrepresented and European scientists continue to be outperformed by North Americans, adding this to the list of issues with today’s global scientific system. They argue that the “increasing burden of knowledge” requires more time in school to learn new skills and also that scientists need “new kinds of institutions to support them” that counterbalance the dangers of “over-specialization” in modern scientific research. They therefore “argue that increases in funding for science provided by agencies that take into account the broad range of skills scientists possess and supports research in a wide range of fields would accelerate progress. So too would universities that open themselves up to new areas of research.”
explanation: The text contains precise statistics and quoted phrases, typical of a formal summary, and its highly uniform, paraphrase-like style is often seen in AI-generated content. However, it could still be genuine research summarization.
